comments:
Summary:
The paper proposes a graph-regularized low-rank representation (GLRR) to learn cleaned/robust feature representations for aerosol optical depth (AOD) retrieval. The method augments a standard LRR model with a Laplacian regularizer constructed from existing satellite physical-retrieval outputs, then feeds the learned coefficients into an SVM regressor. Optimization is performed by a linearized ADMM scheme. Experiments on two satellite datasets (MODIS, MISR) with collocated AERONET ground truth show improvements over a physical retrieval baseline and several empirical regressors (OLS, ridge, NN, SVM) and over LRR+SVM without graph regularization.

Strengths:
- The proposed approach addresses realistic issues in remote sensing data (noise, corruptions, nonlinear local structure) and combines model-driven (physical retrievals) and data-driven cues.
- Mathematical formulation is clear (LRR + graph Laplacian), and an optimization algorithm is provided with implementation-level details.
- Experiments use two different sensors and real AERONET collocations. Comparisons include a reasonable set of baselines and include parameter sensitivity analysis.

Weaknesses and concerns:
1. Novelty: the contribution is incremental — it combines established components (LRR, graph Laplacian regularizer, using external outputs to build graphs, SVM regression). The novelty is in this particular combination and the application to AOD retrieval, not in fundamentally new methodology.
2. Details missing or unclear: the paper does not state some important settings (e.g., the chosen k for k-NN when building W, the chosen σ for the heat kernel, computation time and complexity, and practical guidance for parameter selection beyond the selection ranges). It is said that λ and β are chosen by 5-fold CV but the final selected values and sensitivity beyond the narrow plots are not thoroughly discussed.
3. Dataset size and generality: the collocated datasets are relatively small (843 for MODIS, 1045 for MISR). The paper does not discuss whether results generalize to larger/other regions, or how sensitive the method is to the quality of the physical retrieval used to build the graph.
4. Baselines and ablation: while several baselines are used, modern regressors (e.g., ensemble methods, more advanced deep models) are not considered. More importantly, an ablation study isolating the contribution of (a) LRR denoising alone, (b) graph regularization with different graphs (e.g., spectral features or raw radiances), and (c) varying quality of the physical retrieval used to construct the graph would strengthen claims that the particular use of satellite retrievals as a graph prior is effective.
5. Statistical rigor: results report averages over repeated splits but lack statistical significance tests (confidence intervals, p-values). The improvement magnitude in some cases seems modest; statistical tests would help.
6. Presentation issues: the provided text contains duplicated and garbled paragraphs toward the end; proofreading and cleanup are required. Some derivation steps (the linearization and approximation used in the Z-update) are compact and would benefit from clearer justification or references to convergence properties of the chosen linearized ADMM variant.
7. Convergence and initialization: more discussion on convergence behavior (number of iterations until convergence, sensitivity to initialization) and stability would be useful.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Provide full implementation details: k and σ choices for graph construction, final hyperparameters used in experiments, and runtime/complexity measurements.
- Add ablation experiments: compare (i) raw features + SVM, (ii) LRR+SVM, (iii) GLRR+SVM with graphs built from physical retrievals and from alternative signals, showing the separate contribution of each component. Also evaluate robustness to noisy/biased physical retrievals used to build the graph.
- Include more diverse regression baselines (e.g., Random Forests, Gradient Boosting, or a simple deep learning baseline) to better position performance among modern empirical methods.
- Report statistical significance and confidence intervals for reported metrics.
- Clean up the manuscript formatting, remove duplicate text, and expand explanation of the linearization step and any convergence guarantees (or empirical convergence curves).
- Consider releasing code and trained models to facilitate reproducibility.

Conclusion:
The paper presents a sensible and practically relevant application of existing representation-learning techniques to the AOD retrieval problem. Results are encouraging but incremental. With added experiments to address the concerns above and a cleaned, more detailed presentation, the work would be a solid contribution to the literature on empirical satellite AOD retrieval methods.
